comment_text.sn12.33:1.2: “Grounds for knowledge” (ñāṇavatthu) are those things whose understanding leads to liberation.
comment_text.sn12.33:5.1: Dhamme ñāṇaṁ is the knowledge of the principles of the teaching that are apparent in the present life (cf. diṭṭheva dhamme). It contrasts with anvaye ñāṇaṁ, knowledge inferred to the past and future. The pair, along with knowledge of others’ minds and conventional knowledge, are also at DN 33:1.11.103, DN 34:1.5.35, and Vb 16:307.1. The two forms of knowledge are illustrated in the narrative of Vipassī (DN 14:1.36.1).
comment_text.sn12.33:5.2: The same phrase is found in SN 42.11:2.9, which likewise discusses what is presently observable and what is inferred. Similar terms also describe one who realizes the Dhamma as a stream-enterer (MN 56:18.8). | Akālikena pattena is a single phrase (“immediately attained”) referring to realization in the present.
comment_text.sn12.33:7.2: This knowledge “inferred” from the principles of the teaching allows Sāriputta to state that there has never been, nor ever will be, anyone more enlightened than the Buddha, even though he cannot read the minds of all the Buddhas (SN 47.12:6.2, DN 16:1.17.8, DN 28:2.2). Pasenadi likewise “infers from the teaching” that the Buddha is awakened due to his behavior and that of the Saṅgha; since Pasenadi was not enlightened, this is not a special faculty of awakening (MN 89:13.17). Nonetheless, a fool like Sunakkhatta cannot infer in this way (MN 12:5.1). In all of these cases, dhamma has the primary meaning of “teaching”, but in particular, the principles of the teaching that are observable (see especially DN 28:2.14). See too SN 42.11, where Bhadraka is led to infer from his present experience to the past and future.

root_text.sn12.33:1.3: taṁ suṇātha, sādhukaṁ manasi karotha, bhāsissāmī”ti.
root_text.sn12.33:1.4: “Evaṁ, bhante”ti kho te bhikkhū bhagavato paccassosuṁ.
root_text.sn12.33:2.2: Jarāmaraṇe ñāṇaṁ, jarāmaraṇasamudaye ñāṇaṁ, jarāmaraṇanirodhe ñāṇaṁ, jarāmaraṇanirodhagāminiyā paṭipadāya ñāṇaṁ;
root_text.sn12.33:2.3: jātiyā ñāṇaṁ, jātisamudaye ñāṇaṁ, jātinirodhe ñāṇaṁ, jātinirodhagāminiyā paṭipadāya ñāṇaṁ;
root_text.sn12.33:2.4: bhave ñāṇaṁ, bhavasamudaye ñāṇaṁ, bhavanirodhe ñāṇaṁ, bhavanirodhagāminiyā paṭipadāya ñāṇaṁ;
root_text.sn12.33:2.5: upādāne ñāṇaṁ, upādānasamudaye ñāṇaṁ, upādānanirodhe ñāṇaṁ, upādānanirodhagāminiyā paṭipadāya ñāṇaṁ;
root_text.sn12.33:2.6: taṇhāya ñāṇaṁ, taṇhāsamudaye ñāṇaṁ, taṇhānirodhe ñāṇaṁ, taṇhānirodhagāminiyā paṭipadāya ñāṇaṁ;
root_text.sn12.33:2.7: vedanāya ñāṇaṁ, vedanāsamudaye ñāṇaṁ, vedanānirodhe ñāṇaṁ, vedanānirodhagāminiyā paṭipadāya ñāṇaṁ;
root_text.sn12.33:2.12: saṅkhāresu ñāṇaṁ, saṅkhārasamudaye ñāṇaṁ, saṅkhāranirodhe ñāṇaṁ, saṅkhāranirodhagāminiyā paṭipadāya ñāṇaṁ.
root_text.sn12.33:3.2: Yā tesaṁ tesaṁ sattānaṁ tamhi tamhi sattanikāye jarā jīraṇatā khaṇḍiccaṁ pāliccaṁ valittacatā āyuno saṁhāni indriyānaṁ paripāko,
root_text.sn12.33:3.4: Yā tesaṁ tesaṁ sattānaṁ tamhā tamhā sattanikāyā cuti cavanatā bhedo antaradhānaṁ maccu maraṇaṁ kālakiriyā khandhānaṁ bhedo kaḷevarassa nikkhepo.
root_text.sn12.33:4.3: ayameva ariyo aṭṭhaṅgiko maggo jarāmaraṇanirodhagāminī paṭipadā, seyyathidaṁ—
root_text.sn12.33:5.1: Yato kho, bhikkhave, ariyasāvako evaṁ jarāmaraṇaṁ pajānāti, evaṁ jarāmaraṇasamudayaṁ pajānāti, evaṁ jarāmaraṇanirodhaṁ pajānāti, evaṁ jarāmaraṇanirodhagāminiṁ paṭipadaṁ pajānāti, idamassa dhamme ñāṇaṁ.
root_text.sn12.33:5.2: So iminā dhammena diṭṭhena viditena akālikena pattena pariyogāḷhena atītānāgate nayaṁ neti.
root_text.sn12.33:6.1: Ye kho keci atītamaddhānaṁ samaṇā vā brāhmaṇā vā jarāmaraṇaṁ abbhaññaṁsu, jarāmaraṇasamudayaṁ abbhaññaṁsu, jarāmaraṇanirodhaṁ abbhaññaṁsu, jarāmaraṇanirodhagāminiṁ paṭipadaṁ abbhaññaṁsu, sabbete evameva abbhaññaṁsu, seyyathāpāhaṁ etarahi.
root_text.sn12.33:7.1: Yepi hi keci anāgatamaddhānaṁ samaṇā vā brāhmaṇā vā jarāmaraṇaṁ abhijānissanti, jarāmaraṇasamudayaṁ abhijānissanti, jarāmaraṇanirodhaṁ abhijānissanti, jarāmaraṇanirodhagāminiṁ paṭipadaṁ abhijānissanti, sabbete evameva abhijānissanti, seyyathāpāhaṁ etarahīti.
root_text.sn12.33:8.1: Yato kho, bhikkhave, ariyasāvakassa imāni dve ñāṇāni parisuddhāni honti pariyodātāni—
root_text.sn12.33:8.3: Ayaṁ vuccati, bhikkhave, ariyasāvako diṭṭhisampanno itipi, dassanasampanno itipi, āgato imaṁ saddhammaṁ itipi, passati imaṁ saddhammaṁ itipi, sekkhena ñāṇena samannāgato itipi, sekkhāya vijjāya samannāgato itipi, dhammasotaṁ samāpanno itipi, ariyo nibbedhikapañño itipi, amatadvāraṁ āhacca tiṭṭhati itipīti.
root_text.sn12.33:10.3: ayameva ariyo aṭṭhaṅgiko maggo saṅkhāranirodhagāminī paṭipadā, seyyathidaṁ—
root_text.sn12.33:11.1: Yato kho, bhikkhave, ariyasāvako evaṁ saṅkhāre pajānāti, evaṁ saṅkhārasamudayaṁ pajānāti, evaṁ saṅkhāranirodhaṁ pajānāti, evaṁ saṅkhāranirodhagāminiṁ paṭipadaṁ pajānāti, idamassa dhamme ñāṇaṁ.
root_text.sn12.33:11.2: So iminā dhammena diṭṭhena viditena akālikena pattena pariyogāḷhena atītānāgate nayaṁ neti.
root_text.sn12.33:12.1: Ye kho keci atītamaddhānaṁ samaṇā vā brāhmaṇā vā saṅkhāre abbhaññaṁsu, saṅkhārasamudayaṁ abbhaññaṁsu, saṅkhāranirodhaṁ abbhaññaṁsu, saṅkhāranirodhagāminiṁ paṭipadaṁ abbhaññaṁsu, sabbete evameva abbhaññaṁsu, seyyathāpāhaṁ etarahi.
root_text.sn12.33:13.1: Ye hipi keci anāgatamaddhānaṁ samaṇā vā brāhmaṇā vā saṅkhāre abhijānissanti, saṅkhārasamudayaṁ abhijānissanti, saṅkhāranirodhaṁ abhijānissanti, saṅkhāranirodhagāminiṁ paṭipadaṁ abhijānissanti, sabbete evameva abhijānissanti, seyyathāpāhaṁ etarahi.
root_text.sn12.33:14.1: Yato kho, bhikkhave, ariyasāvakassa imāni dve ñāṇāni parisuddhāni honti pariyodātāni—
root_text.sn12.33:14.3: Ayaṁ vuccati, bhikkhave, ariyasāvako diṭṭhisampanno itipi, dassanasampanno itipi, āgato imaṁ saddhammaṁ itipi, passati imaṁ saddhammaṁ itipi, sekkhena ñāṇena samannāgato itipi, sekkhāya vijjāya samannāgato itipi, dhammasotaṁ samāpanno itipi, ariyo nibbedhikapañño itipi, amatadvāraṁ āhacca tiṭṭhati itipī”ti.

translation_text.sn12.33:1.2: “Mendicants, I will teach forty-four grounds for knowledge.
translation_text.sn12.33:1.3: Listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.”
translation_text.sn12.33:2.1: “And what are the forty-four grounds for knowledge?
translation_text.sn12.33:2.2: Knowledge of old age and death, knowledge of the origin of old age and death, knowledge of the cessation of old age and death, and knowledge of the practice that leads to the cessation of old age and death.
translation_text.sn12.33:2.9: Knowledge of the six sense fields …
translation_text.sn12.33:2.12: Knowledge of choices, knowledge of the origin of choices, knowledge of the cessation of choices, and knowledge of the practice that leads to the cessation of choices.
translation_text.sn12.33:2.13: These are called the forty-four grounds for knowledge.
translation_text.sn12.33:3.1: And what is old age and death?
translation_text.sn12.33:3.2: The old age, decrepitude, broken teeth, grey hair, wrinkly skin, diminished vitality, and failing faculties of the various sentient beings in the various orders of sentient beings.
translation_text.sn12.33:3.4: The passing away, passing on, disintegration, demise, mortality, death, decease, breaking up of the aggregates, and laying to rest of the corpse of the various sentient beings in the various orders of sentient beings.
translation_text.sn12.33:3.6: Such is old age, and such is death.
translation_text.sn12.33:3.7: This is called old age and death.
translation_text.sn12.33:4.1: Rebirth is the origin of old age and death.
translation_text.sn12.33:4.2: When rebirth ceases, old age and death cease.
translation_text.sn12.33:4.3: The practice that leads to the cessation of old age and death is simply this noble eightfold path, that is:
translation_text.sn12.33:4.4: right view, right thought, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion.
translation_text.sn12.33:5.1: A noble disciple understands old age and death, their origin, their cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation. This is their knowledge of the teaching.
translation_text.sn12.33:5.2: With this teaching that is seen, understood, immediately attained, and fathomed, they apply the method to the past and future.
translation_text.sn12.33:6.1: Whatever ascetics and brahmins in the past directly knew old age and death, their origin, their cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation, all of them directly knew these things in exactly the same way that I do now.
translation_text.sn12.33:7.1: Whatever ascetics and brahmins in the future will directly know old age and death, their origin, their cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation, all of them will directly know these things in exactly the same way that I do now.
translation_text.sn12.33:8.1: A noble disciple has purified and cleansed these two knowledges—
translation_text.sn12.33:8.2: knowledge of the teaching and inferential knowledge.
translation_text.sn12.33:8.3: When a noble disciple has done this, they’re one who is called ‘one accomplished in view’, ‘one accomplished in vision’, ‘one who has come to the true teaching’, ‘one who sees this true teaching’, ‘one endowed with a trainee’s knowledge’, ‘one who has entered the stream of the teaching’, ‘a noble one with penetrative wisdom’, and also ‘one who stands pressing against the door to freedom from death’.
translation_text.sn12.33:9.7: And what are the six sense fields? …
translation_text.sn12.33:9.8: And what are name and form? …
translation_text.sn12.33:9.11: There are three kinds of choices.
translation_text.sn12.33:9.12: Choices by way of body, speech, and mind.
translation_text.sn12.33:10.1: Ignorance is the origin of choices.
translation_text.sn12.33:10.3: The practice that leads to the cessation of choices is simply this noble eightfold path, that is:
translation_text.sn12.33:10.4: right view, right thought, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion.
translation_text.sn12.33:11.1: A noble disciple understands choices, their origin, their cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation. This is their knowledge of the teaching.
translation_text.sn12.33:11.2: With this teaching that is seen, understood, immediately attained, and fathomed, they apply the method to the past and future.
translation_text.sn12.33:12.1: Whatever ascetics and brahmins in the past directly knew choices, their origin, their cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation, all of them directly knew these things in exactly the same way that I do now.
translation_text.sn12.33:13.1: Whatever ascetics and brahmins in the future will directly know choices, their origin, their cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation, all of them will directly know these things in exactly the same way that I do now.
translation_text.sn12.33:14.1: A noble disciple has purified and cleansed these two knowledges—
translation_text.sn12.33:14.2: knowledge of the teaching and inferential knowledge.
translation_text.sn12.33:14.3: When a noble disciple has done this, they’re one who is called ‘one accomplished in view’, ‘one accomplished in vision’, ‘one who has come to the true teaching’, ‘one who sees this true teaching’, ‘one endowed with a trainee’s knowledge’, ‘one who has entered the stream of the teaching’, ‘a noble one with penetrative wisdom’, and also ‘one who stands pressing against the door to freedom from death’.”

variant_text.sn12.33:2.1: Katamāni → katamāni ca (sya-all, km, pts1ed, pts2ed, mr)
